title: Warfarin — Initiation & Reversal
oneLiner: Anticoagulant used in AF, VTE, mechanical valves; requires careful initiation and emergent reversal for life-threatening bleeding or urgent surgery.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Major haemorrhage** (intracranial, GI, retroperitoneal, compartment syndrome): Rapid fall in Hb/GCS, melaena/haematemesis, unexplained hypotension despite fluids. **Single finding:** NEWS2 ≥5 *or* acute focal neurology with INR >3. Do not wait for INR to check — start reversal immediately if clinical suspicion high.
- **Thromboembolic recurrence** due to *overly aggressive* reversal in low-risk bleeding (e.g. minor epistaxis in mechanical mitral valve): **Single finding:** mechanical valve click absent or new ischaemic limb pain. Reversal of INR without clear major bleeding can be fatal. Balance risk.
sections.Presentation:
- **Initiation settings:** New AF (paroxysmal/persistent), DVT/PE, mechanical heart valve, antiphospholipid syndrome, LV thrombus.
- **Reversal settings:** Overt bleeding (GI, intracranial, haematuria, haemoptysis), high INR on routine check (asymptomatic), preoperative planning, trauma.
- **Symptoms of overcoagulation:** Easy bruising, prolonged bleeding from cuts, dark stools, vomiting "coffee grounds", severe headache (intracranial), back/flank pain (retroperitoneal), limb swelling/compartment syndrome.
- **Symptoms of undercoagulation (failure/breakthrough):** Dyspnoea, chest pain (PE), leg swelling/pain (DVT), stroke/TIA.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Coagulopathy from other causes:** Liver disease (elevated bilirubin, low albumin, spider naevi) – differentiate with LFTs, normal INR on stopping warfarin (if safe).
- **Vitamin K deficiency (malnutrition, antibiotics, cirrhosis):** Low factor VII (but warfarin also declines factors II, IX, X). Check mixing studies? Not ward.
- **DIC (disseminated intravascular coagulation):** Look for schistocytes on film, prolonged APTT and PT, low fibrinogen, elevated D-dimer.
- **Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) confusion:** If patient also on heparin — check platelets, HIT antibody. Warfarin can cause venous limb gangrene if started in acute HIT.
- **Direct oral anticoagulant (DOAC) effect:** No INR elevation typical, but can be prolonged; specific assays (anti-Xa for rivaroxaban/apixaban, dilute thrombin time for dabigatran). **Splitter:** DOAC INR is rarely >3 unless extreme overdose; warfarin INR >3 is common therapeutic.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Capillary blood glucose (if neurological signs), ECG (new AF vs. rate control), urine dip (haematuria).
- **Bloods:**
  - **INR** – baseline before starting, then daily for first few days of initiation.
  - **FBC** – baseline, to detect occult bleeding (falling Hb).
  - **LFTs/U+E** – especially for initiation (hepatic impairment affects warfarin metabolism).
  - **Coagulation screen:** PT, APTT, fibrinogen in major bleeding (to rule out DIC if INR not explained by warfarin).
  - **Blood group and crossmatch** if major bleeding.
- **Imaging:**
  - **CT head** (urgent, non‑contrast) – if any neurological symptom + on warfarin.
  - **CT abdomen** (if unexplained hypotension, abdominal pain, falling Hb) – retroperitoneal bleed.
  - **US/Doppler leg** if suspected DVT recurrence.
  - **Chest X-ray** if haemoptysis or suspected PE.
- **Special tests:**
  - **Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) antibodies** if platelets drop >50% on heparin.
  - **Mixing studies** only if INR does not correct with vitamin K (rare factor inhibitors).
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring systems:**
  - **INR therapeutic range:** 2.0–3.0 (most indications), 2.5–3.5 (mechanical mitral valve, some guidelines).
  - **Major bleeding definition:** fatal, symptomatic in critical organ (intracranial, pericardial, retroperitoneal), Hb drop ≥20 g/L, need for ≥2 units RBC transfusion.
- **Never-miss test:** **INR** before any warfarin dose change or before reversal. **CT head** in any patient on warfarin with new headache, seizure, confusion, or focal neurology.
sections.Management:
#### Immediate (first hour / ED)
- **If major bleeding:** Stop warfarin immediately. Call haematology and local blood bank. Give:
  - **Prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC) e.g. Beriplex [check local dose]** – 25–50 IU/kg IV. Usual dose: 2000–3000 IU.
  - **Vitamin K (Konakion) 5–10 mg IV slow (over 1–2 min)** – wait for PCC to take effect (15–30 min). Do not use IM (haematoma risk).
  - **If no PCC available:** Fresh frozen plasma (FFP) 15–20 mL/kg IV (less effective, takes longer, risk of fluid overload).
  - **Supportive:** IV fluids, blood transfusion as per major haemorrhage protocol.
- **If life-threatening bleed + high INR but no time for INR:** Treat empirically if clinical suspicion high.
- **If non‑major bleeding (e.g. epistaxis, haematuria, minor GI):** Omit next dose. Check INR. If INR >8: give oral vitamin K (Konakion) 5 mg (or IV if unable to swallow). If INR <8: consider local pressure, no reversal unless actively bleeding.
- **If asymptomatic high INR (e.g. >8 on routine check):** Omit warfarin. Give oral vitamin K 5 mg. Recheck INR 24h. Do not give PCC unless bleeding develops.

#### Ward (first 24–72h)
- **Initiation of warfarin:**
  - Confirm no contraindications (active bleeding, recent surgery within 48h, coagulopathy, uncontrolled hypertension).
  - **Loading regimen:** [check local policy] – e.g. Fennerty regimen: 5 mg or 10 mg daily for first 2–3 days, then daily monitoring + dose adjustment.
  - **Bridge with heparin/LMWH** (e.g. enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg once daily for treatment dose, or 1 mg/kg twice daily; or unfractionated heparin) until INR 2.0–3.0 for at least 24 hours (except mechanical valves need longer overlap). For AF with no VTE: usually no bridge needed.
  - **Monitor INR daily** during loading.
  - **Check baseline FBC, LFTs, U+E.**
  - **Counsel patient:** green book, food interactions (leafy greens, cranberry, alcohol), need for regular monitoring, signs of bleeding/bruising.
  - **Record indication and target INR** clearly.
- **Reversal continued:**
  - After PCC + vitamin K, recheck INR 30–60 min. If still >1.5 and bleeding continues, give additional PCC [dose per local protocol].
  - Document indication for reversal and plan for restarting warfarin (usually after bleeding source secured, e.g. 7–14 days, with bridging if high thromboembolic risk).

#### Long-term / Outpatient
- **Monitoring:** INR check interval – usual weekly for stable, then monthly once stable. Anytime change in interacting medication (antibiotics, antifungals, amiodarone, NSAIDs) or diet.
- **Target INR:**
  - AF / VTE / LV thrombus: 2.0–3.0.
  - Mechanical mitral valve / bicaval valve: 2.5–3.5.
  - Mechanical aortic valve + risk factors: 2.0–3.0 (some 2.5–3.5).
- **Self-monitoring:** for selected patients (stable, motivated).
- **Intercurrent illness:** Sick day rules – if vomiting/diarrhoea/dehydration, reduce dose or omit and check INR.

#### Default Orders (if admitted)
- **Fluids:** IV crystalloid (e.g. Hartmann’s) at 80–100 mL/h if bleeding or volume depletion; avoid if fluid overload.
- **VTE prophylaxis:** Not needed when on therapeutic warfarin; if bridging with LMWH, continue that.
- **Diet:** Full as tolerated (but avoid large changes in vitamin K intake). If NBM, give IV vitamin K prophylactically? Usually not required for short NBM.
- **Observation frequency:** 4‑hourly NEWS2 if stable; if after reversal, consider 1‑hourly for first 4 hours.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Warfarin (initiation):** [check local policy] – e.g. 5 mg or 10 mg oral once daily for days 1–2, then adjust based on INR. Common UK regimen: 10 mg day 1, 5 mg day 2, then titrate. Never give a double dose.
- **Vitamin K (reversal):**
  - **Non‑urgent (no bleeding, INR >8):** 5 mg oral (Konakion oral drops or tablets). Onset 12–24h.
  - **Urgent (major bleeding):** 5–10 mg IV slow (over 1–2 min) – may repeat once if INR still high after PCC.
- **Prothrombin Complex Concentrate (PCC):** [check local dose product – Beriplex, Octaplex, etc.] Typical: 25–50 IU/kg IV (maximum 3000 IU per dose). Given with IV vitamin K.
- **FFP:** Only if PCC unavailable – 15–20 mL/kg IV. Slower, less effective.
- **Bridge therapy:**
  - Enoxaparin (treatment dose) – 1.5 mg/kg SC once daily (CrCl ≥30) or 1 mg/kg SC twice daily (e.g. in higher thrombotic risk). Adjust for renal impairment.
  - Unfractionated heparin IV – with APTT monitoring.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Active bleeding, recent major surgery/trauma, uncontrolled hypertension (BP >180/110), severe liver disease, concurrent strong CYP inhibitors (amiodarone, fluconazole, metronidazole, ciprofloxacin, NSAIDs) – increase INR. Inducers (carbamazepine, rifampicin) – decrease INR.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - **Hepatic:** Warfarin dose may need reduction; check LFTs. Reversal – vitamin K and PCC still used but caution in severe cirrhosis (PCC may contain factors not deficient).
  - **Renal:** Warfarin clearance unaffected, but bridging LMWH needs dose adjustment (enoxaparin 1 mg/kg once daily if CrCl 30–50; avoid if CrCl <30? Use UFH).
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Giving vitamin K in non‑urgent high INR without checking bleeding status** – can over‑reverse and cause thromboembolism.
  - **Not checking INR before giving loading dose day 2** – leading to supratherapeutic INR if patient has renal/hepatic impairment.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Major bleeding (intracranial, GI, retroperitoneal, pericardial).
  - Warfarin‑induced skin necrosis (rare, occurs within first week – painful necrotic plaques on fatty areas) – stop warfarin, give vitamin K, switch to LMWH.
  - Purple toes syndrome (cholesterol emboli) – on warfarin >2 weeks, purple discolouration of toes.
  - HIT if concurrent heparin (though warfarin can cause venous gangrene if started in acute HIT without adequate heparin bridging).
  - Drug interactions (antibiotics, antifungals, amiodarone) leading to sudden INR spike.
- **Chronic:**
  - Osteoporosis with long‑term use (warfarin inhibits vitamin K‑dependent bone proteins).
  - Suboptimal time in therapeutic range (TTR) increases risk of both thrombosis and bleeding.
  - Need for regular monitoring and dietary restrictions (reduced quality of life).
sections.Prognosis:
- **Major bleeding:** Mortality ~10–20% for intracranial, lower for GI if controlled rapidly with PCC. **Time to reversal is critical** – delay >1 hour increases mortality.
- **Initiation:** With good loading and bridging, risk of recurrent VTE <1% in first month. TTR >70% is associated with low annual risk of bleeding (<2% major) and thrombosis (<1%).
- **Warfarin in AF:** Reduces stroke by ~65% compared to placebo; risk of major bleeding ~2–5% per year.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always perform a structured bleeding risk assessment (e.g., HAS-BLED) before starting** – but do not use it to contraindicate warfarin; it guides modifiable risk factors (BP control, avoid NSAIDs/alcohol).
- **Document target INR and indication clearly** on drug chart and in notes – reduces dosing errors.
- **Check INR on day 2 of loading** – if INR >1.5, omit next dose; if >2.0, consider lower maintenance dose.
- **Use ITU-level monitoring** for any patient who receives PCC – risk of thromboembolism (MI, stroke) in 1–2% over 7 days.
- **If patient on warfarin + dual antiplatelet (triple therapy)**, discuss with cardiology/haematology for reduced warfarin target and shorter triple therapy duration.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not give vitamin K alone for major bleeding** – you need PCC for immediate reversal; vitamin K alone takes 12h+.
- **Do not give FFP as first line if PCC is available** – FFP is less effective, has higher volume load, and takes longer.
- **Do not restart warfarin too soon after reversal** without securing source of bleeding and consulting specialist.
- **Do not start warfarin in acute VTE without adequate heparin bridge (≥3 days of therapeutic LMWH)** – warfarin can transiently produce a procoagulant state (protein C and S decline first) leading to thrombosis expansion.
- **Do not assume INR falls quickly after withholding warfarin** – half‑life 40h; vitamin K may be needed if INR >8.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Any new bleeding (black stools, blood in urine, vomiting blood, unexplained bruising, epistaxis >15min).
  - Severe headache, visual changes, weakness, slurred speech, seizure.
  - New severe abdominal/back/flank pain.
  - Persistent vomiting or diarrhoea (affects INR).
- **Follow-up:**
  - INR check within 1–3 days after any dose change.
  - Warfarin review with GP or anticoagulation clinic within 1 week of discharge.
  - If reversal occurred (vitamin K given), INR may drop and need re‑check after 48h to ensure it is rebounding (vitamin K can cause warfarin resistance for days).
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **NEWS2 ≥5** – immediate review by FY2/reg.
- **NEWS2 ≥7 or lactate >4** – consider ITU referral and massive transfusion protocol.
- **GCS drop by ≥2 or any neurological deficit** – urgent CT head, haematology reg, neurosurgery consult at hospital with neurosurgical cover (transfer may be needed).
- **Suspected major bleeding (haemodynamic instability, Hb drop >20 g/L, need for vasopressors)** – call reg, haematology, blood bank; prepare for PCC.
- **Anaphylaxis to PCC or vitamin K** – stop drug, call crash team, give adrenaline IM.

rawMarkdown:
## Warfarin — Initiation & Reversal

**One-liner:** Anticoagulant used in AF, VTE, mechanical valves; requires careful initiation and emergent reversal for life-threatening bleeding or urgent surgery.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Major haemorrhage** (intracranial, GI, retroperitoneal, compartment syndrome): Rapid fall in Hb/GCS, melaena/haematemesis, unexplained hypotension despite fluids. **Single finding:** NEWS2 ≥5 *or* acute focal neurology with INR >3. Do not wait for INR to check — start reversal immediately if clinical suspicion high.
- **Thromboembolic recurrence** due to *overly aggressive* reversal in low-risk bleeding (e.g. minor epistaxis in mechanical mitral valve): **Single finding:** mechanical valve click absent or new ischaemic limb pain. Reversal of INR without clear major bleeding can be fatal. Balance risk.

### Presentation
- **Initiation settings:** New AF (paroxysmal/persistent), DVT/PE, mechanical heart valve, antiphospholipid syndrome, LV thrombus.
- **Reversal settings:** Overt bleeding (GI, intracranial, haematuria, haemoptysis), high INR on routine check (asymptomatic), preoperative planning, trauma.
- **Symptoms of overcoagulation:** Easy bruising, prolonged bleeding from cuts, dark stools, vomiting "coffee grounds", severe headache (intracranial), back/flank pain (retroperitoneal), limb swelling/compartment syndrome.
- **Symptoms of undercoagulation (failure/breakthrough):** Dyspnoea, chest pain (PE), leg swelling/pain (DVT), stroke/TIA.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Coagulopathy from other causes:** Liver disease (elevated bilirubin, low albumin, spider naevi) – differentiate with LFTs, normal INR on stopping warfarin (if safe).
- **Vitamin K deficiency (malnutrition, antibiotics, cirrhosis):** Low factor VII (but warfarin also declines factors II, IX, X). Check mixing studies? Not ward.
- **DIC (disseminated intravascular coagulation):** Look for schistocytes on film, prolonged APTT and PT, low fibrinogen, elevated D-dimer.
- **Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) confusion:** If patient also on heparin — check platelets, HIT antibody. Warfarin can cause venous limb gangrene if started in acute HIT.
- **Direct oral anticoagulant (DOAC) effect:** No INR elevation typical, but can be prolonged; specific assays (anti-Xa for rivaroxaban/apixaban, dilute thrombin time for dabigatran). **Splitter:** DOAC INR is rarely >3 unless extreme overdose; warfarin INR >3 is common therapeutic.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Capillary blood glucose (if neurological signs), ECG (new AF vs. rate control), urine dip (haematuria).
- **Bloods:**
  - **INR** – baseline before starting, then daily for first few days of initiation.
  - **FBC** – baseline, to detect occult bleeding (falling Hb).
  - **LFTs/U+E** – especially for initiation (hepatic impairment affects warfarin metabolism).
  - **Coagulation screen:** PT, APTT, fibrinogen in major bleeding (to rule out DIC if INR not explained by warfarin).
  - **Blood group and crossmatch** if major bleeding.
- **Imaging:**
  - **CT head** (urgent, non‑contrast) – if any neurological symptom + on warfarin.
  - **CT abdomen** (if unexplained hypotension, abdominal pain, falling Hb) – retroperitoneal bleed.
  - **US/Doppler leg** if suspected DVT recurrence.
  - **Chest X-ray** if haemoptysis or suspected PE.
- **Special tests:**
  - **Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) antibodies** if platelets drop >50% on heparin.
  - **Mixing studies** only if INR does not correct with vitamin K (rare factor inhibitors).
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring systems:**
  - **INR therapeutic range:** 2.0–3.0 (most indications), 2.5–3.5 (mechanical mitral valve, some guidelines).
  - **Major bleeding definition:** fatal, symptomatic in critical organ (intracranial, pericardial, retroperitoneal), Hb drop ≥20 g/L, need for ≥2 units RBC transfusion.
- **Never-miss test:** **INR** before any warfarin dose change or before reversal. **CT head** in any patient on warfarin with new headache, seizure, confusion, or focal neurology.

### Management
#### Immediate (first hour / ED)
- **If major bleeding:** Stop warfarin immediately. Call haematology and local blood bank. Give:
  - **Prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC) e.g. Beriplex [check local dose]** – 25–50 IU/kg IV. Usual dose: 2000–3000 IU.
  - **Vitamin K (Konakion) 5–10 mg IV slow (over 1–2 min)** – wait for PCC to take effect (15–30 min). Do not use IM (haematoma risk).
  - **If no PCC available:** Fresh frozen plasma (FFP) 15–20 mL/kg IV (less effective, takes longer, risk of fluid overload).
  - **Supportive:** IV fluids, blood transfusion as per major haemorrhage protocol.
- **If life-threatening bleed + high INR but no time for INR:** Treat empirically if clinical suspicion high.
- **If non‑major bleeding (e.g. epistaxis, haematuria, minor GI):** Omit next dose. Check INR. If INR >8: give oral vitamin K (Konakion) 5 mg (or IV if unable to swallow). If INR <8: consider local pressure, no reversal unless actively bleeding.
- **If asymptomatic high INR (e.g. >8 on routine check):** Omit warfarin. Give oral vitamin K 5 mg. Recheck INR 24h. Do not give PCC unless bleeding develops.

#### Ward (first 24–72h)
- **Initiation of warfarin:**
  - Confirm no contraindications (active bleeding, recent surgery within 48h, coagulopathy, uncontrolled hypertension).
  - **Loading regimen:** [check local policy] – e.g. Fennerty regimen: 5 mg or 10 mg daily for first 2–3 days, then daily monitoring + dose adjustment.
  - **Bridge with heparin/LMWH** (e.g. enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg once daily for treatment dose, or 1 mg/kg twice daily; or unfractionated heparin) until INR 2.0–3.0 for at least 24 hours (except mechanical valves need longer overlap). For AF with no VTE: usually no bridge needed.
  - **Monitor INR daily** during loading.
  - **Check baseline FBC, LFTs, U+E.**
  - **Counsel patient:** green book, food interactions (leafy greens, cranberry, alcohol), need for regular monitoring, signs of bleeding/bruising.
  - **Record indication and target INR** clearly.
- **Reversal continued:**
  - After PCC + vitamin K, recheck INR 30–60 min. If still >1.5 and bleeding continues, give additional PCC [dose per local protocol].
  - Document indication for reversal and plan for restarting warfarin (usually after bleeding source secured, e.g. 7–14 days, with bridging if high thromboembolic risk).

#### Long-term / Outpatient
- **Monitoring:** INR check interval – usual weekly for stable, then monthly once stable. Anytime change in interacting medication (antibiotics, antifungals, amiodarone, NSAIDs) or diet.
- **Target INR:**
  - AF / VTE / LV thrombus: 2.0–3.0.
  - Mechanical mitral valve / bicaval valve: 2.5–3.5.
  - Mechanical aortic valve + risk factors: 2.0–3.0 (some 2.5–3.5).
- **Self-monitoring:** for selected patients (stable, motivated).
- **Intercurrent illness:** Sick day rules – if vomiting/diarrhoea/dehydration, reduce dose or omit and check INR.

#### Default Orders (if admitted)
- **Fluids:** IV crystalloid (e.g. Hartmann’s) at 80–100 mL/h if bleeding or volume depletion; avoid if fluid overload.
- **VTE prophylaxis:** Not needed when on therapeutic warfarin; if bridging with LMWH, continue that.
- **Diet:** Full as tolerated (but avoid large changes in vitamin K intake). If NBM, give IV vitamin K prophylactically? Usually not required for short NBM.
- **Observation frequency:** 4‑hourly NEWS2 if stable; if after reversal, consider 1‑hourly for first 4 hours.

### Drugs & Doses
- **Warfarin (initiation):** [check local policy] – e.g. 5 mg or 10 mg oral once daily for days 1–2, then adjust based on INR. Common UK regimen: 10 mg day 1, 5 mg day 2, then titrate. Never give a double dose.
- **Vitamin K (reversal):**
  - **Non‑urgent (no bleeding, INR >8):** 5 mg oral (Konakion oral drops or tablets). Onset 12–24h.
  - **Urgent (major bleeding):** 5–10 mg IV slow (over 1–2 min) – may repeat once if INR still high after PCC.
- **Prothrombin Complex Concentrate (PCC):** [check local dose product – Beriplex, Octaplex, etc.] Typical: 25–50 IU/kg IV (maximum 3000 IU per dose). Given with IV vitamin K.
- **FFP:** Only if PCC unavailable – 15–20 mL/kg IV. Slower, less effective.
- **Bridge therapy:**
  - Enoxaparin (treatment dose) – 1.5 mg/kg SC once daily (CrCl ≥30) or 1 mg/kg SC twice daily (e.g. in higher thrombotic risk). Adjust for renal impairment.
  - Unfractionated heparin IV – with APTT monitoring.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Active bleeding, recent major surgery/trauma, uncontrolled hypertension (BP >180/110), severe liver disease, concurrent strong CYP inhibitors (amiodarone, fluconazole, metronidazole, ciprofloxacin, NSAIDs) – increase INR. Inducers (carbamazepine, rifampicin) – decrease INR.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - **Hepatic:** Warfarin dose may need reduction; check LFTs. Reversal – vitamin K and PCC still used but caution in severe cirrhosis (PCC may contain factors not deficient).
  - **Renal:** Warfarin clearance unaffected, but bridging LMWH needs dose adjustment (enoxaparin 1 mg/kg once daily if CrCl 30–50; avoid if CrCl <30? Use UFH).
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Giving vitamin K in non‑urgent high INR without checking bleeding status** – can over‑reverse and cause thromboembolism.
  - **Not checking INR before giving loading dose day 2** – leading to supratherapeutic INR if patient has renal/hepatic impairment.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Major bleeding (intracranial, GI, retroperitoneal, pericardial).
  - Warfarin‑induced skin necrosis (rare, occurs within first week – painful necrotic plaques on fatty areas) – stop warfarin, give vitamin K, switch to LMWH.
  - Purple toes syndrome (cholesterol emboli) – on warfarin >2 weeks, purple discolouration of toes.
  - HIT if concurrent heparin (though warfarin can cause venous gangrene if started in acute HIT without adequate heparin bridging).
  - Drug interactions (antibiotics, antifungals, amiodarone) leading to sudden INR spike.
- **Chronic:**
  - Osteoporosis with long‑term use (warfarin inhibits vitamin K‑dependent bone proteins).
  - Suboptimal time in therapeutic range (TTR) increases risk of both thrombosis and bleeding.
  - Need for regular monitoring and dietary restrictions (reduced quality of life).

### Prognosis
- **Major bleeding:** Mortality ~10–20% for intracranial, lower for GI if controlled rapidly with PCC. **Time to reversal is critical** – delay >1 hour increases mortality.
- **Initiation:** With good loading and bridging, risk of recurrent VTE <1% in first month. TTR >70% is associated with low annual risk of bleeding (<2% major) and thrombosis (<1%).
- **Warfarin in AF:** Reduces stroke by ~65% compared to placebo; risk of major bleeding ~2–5% per year.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always perform a structured bleeding risk assessment (e.g., HAS-BLED) before starting** – but do not use it to contraindicate warfarin; it guides modifiable risk factors (BP control, avoid NSAIDs/alcohol).
- **Document target INR and indication clearly** on drug chart and in notes – reduces dosing errors.
- **Check INR on day 2 of loading** – if INR >1.5, omit next dose; if >2.0, consider lower maintenance dose.
- **Use ITU-level monitoring** for any patient who receives PCC – risk of thromboembolism (MI, stroke) in 1–2% over 7 days.
- **If patient on warfarin + dual antiplatelet (triple therapy)**, discuss with cardiology/haematology for reduced warfarin target and shorter triple therapy duration.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not give vitamin K alone for major bleeding** – you need PCC for immediate reversal; vitamin K alone takes 12h+.
- **Do not give FFP as first line if PCC is available** – FFP is less effective, has higher volume load, and takes longer.
- **Do not restart warfarin too soon after reversal** without securing source of bleeding and consulting specialist.
- **Do not start warfarin in acute VTE without adequate heparin bridge (≥3 days of therapeutic LMWH)** – warfarin can transiently produce a procoagulant state (protein C and S decline first) leading to thrombosis expansion.
- **Do not assume INR falls quickly after withholding warfarin** – half‑life 40h; vitamin K may be needed if INR >8.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Any new bleeding (black stools, blood in urine, vomiting blood, unexplained bruising, epistaxis >15min).
  - Severe headache, visual changes, weakness, slurred speech, seizure.
  - New severe abdominal/back/flank pain.
  - Persistent vomiting or diarrhoea (affects INR).
- **Follow-up:**
  - INR check within 1–3 days after any dose change.
  - Warfarin review with GP or anticoagulation clinic within 1 week of discharge.
  - If reversal occurred (vitamin K given), INR may drop and need re‑check after 48h to ensure it is rebounding (vitamin K can cause warfarin resistance for days).

### Escalation Criteria
- **NEWS2 ≥5** – immediate review by FY2/reg.
- **NEWS2 ≥7 or lactate >4** – consider ITU referral and massive transfusion protocol.
- **GCS drop by ≥2 or any neurological deficit** – urgent CT head, haematology reg, neurosurgery consult at hospital with neurosurgical cover (transfer may be needed).
- **Suspected major bleeding (haemodynamic instability, Hb drop >20 g/L, need for vasopressors)** – call reg, haematology, blood bank; prepare for PCC.
- **Anaphylaxis to PCC or vitamin K** – stop drug, call crash team, give adrenaline IM.
